question: Tell us about a time you failed at something important to you. What did you take from it?
lookingFor: Honest ownership of a real setback, a concrete response, and genuine reflection — growth over excuses.

bullets: Name a genuine failure, not a disguised strength
Take clear ownership without blaming circumstances or others
Show the specific change you made and what came from it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I trained for months for a regional science olympiad and placed outside the top twenty. It stung partly because I'd told a lot of people I'd do well, and I had to sit with that. When I went back through the questions I'd dropped, I realised I'd crammed facts but never tested myself under timed conditions, so I froze on things I actually knew. I restructured how I prepared for everything after that — shorter revision blocks, more practice tests, less re-reading. The next competition I placed third. The failure was more useful than a medal would have been.

commonMistakes: Choosing a 'failure' that is obviously a strength in disguise
Spending most of the answer on the failure and rushing the recovery
Blaming external factors rather than owning what was in your control
followUps: What would you do differently if you faced the same situation today?

question: What has been your biggest academic challenge, and how did you work through it?
lookingFor: A real struggle handled with initiative — evidence of persistence and the ability to seek help when needed.

bullets: Describe the challenge specifically, not vaguely
Show what you actively did — not just that time fixed it
Connect it to a habit or approach you carry forward
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Statistics nearly derailed my A-level year. I understood individual concepts fine but kept losing the thread of which test to apply and when, and I panicked in past-paper practice. Rather than just re-reading my notes, I made a decision-tree poster that hung above my desk, and I started doing one timed question every morning before school. I also asked my teacher to mark one rough attempt per week rather than waiting for formal submissions. Over about six weeks the pattern clicked, and I ended up with one of my highest module scores. It taught me that confusion is usually a signal to change the method, not just work harder.

commonMistakes: Describing a challenge so minor it doesn't read as a real struggle
Saying 'I just worked harder' with no specific action
Failing to show what the experience taught you going forward
followUps: Is there a subject or skill you're still working to improve?

question: Describe a time you received feedback that was hard to hear. How did you respond?
lookingFor: Emotional maturity — the ability to sit with criticism, evaluate it honestly, and act on what's useful.

bullets: Be honest that the feedback stung — don't pretend it didn't
Show that you evaluated it rather than accepted or rejected it outright
Describe the concrete change you made and whether it helped
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My drama teacher told me in front of the group that I was technically solid but emotionally flat — that audiences weren't connecting with me. My instinct was to argue, because I'd worked so hard on the technical side. Instead I sat with it for a few days and watched a recording of the scene, and I could see exactly what she meant. I started practising with a friend and asked her to stop me every time I went mechanical. My performance in the end-of-year show felt completely different — more present. I'm glad I didn't dismiss the feedback just because it was uncomfortable.

commonMistakes: Claiming the feedback turned out to be wrong
Showing defensiveness without any genuine reflection
Skipping over how the feedback actually felt and jumping straight to resolution
followUps: How do you now decide which feedback to act on and which to set aside?

question: Have you ever seriously doubted whether you were good enough for something you were pursuing? How did you handle it?
lookingFor: Honest vulnerability paired with the self-awareness and strategies to push through rather than quit.

bullets: Admit the doubt was real — avoid glossing over it
Identify what triggered it specifically
Show the concrete step that helped you move forward, not just willpower
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: When I looked at the portfolio submissions for the art foundation course I wanted, I genuinely thought I wasn't in the same league. I considered not applying at all, which I now find embarrassing. What helped was talking to a mentor who pointed out I was comparing my early drafts to other people's finished work. I shifted my focus to finishing three strong pieces rather than second-guessing all twenty, and I got in. I still doubt myself in new situations, but I'm quicker now to spot when I'm comparing unfairly.

commonMistakes: Presenting self-doubt as something you've permanently solved
Framing it so the doubt was obviously unfounded from the start
Saying 'I just believed in myself' with nothing more concrete
followUps: What do you do now when that feeling comes back?

question: Tell us about a time you had too much on your plate. How did you manage?
lookingFor: Practical prioritisation skills and self-awareness about limits — evidence you won't simply burn out.

bullets: Set the scene with the specific competing demands
Describe the trade-offs you made and how you decided what to drop or delay
Show that you learned a system or habit, not just that you survived
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In the autumn term of my final year I was running the school newspaper, preparing a scholarship application, and trying to keep my predicted grades up. I was doing everything badly. I sat down one Sunday and listed everything I had, roughly scored each by urgency and what the consequences of a lower effort would actually be, and made a decision to hand the newspaper's layout work to a deputy for six weeks. That was the right call — the paper ran fine, and I had the bandwidth to do the scholarship properly. I now do that triage exercise before every busy stretch.

commonMistakes: Describing a stressful period without showing any active decision-making
Making it sound like you simply worked longer hours
Not acknowledging anything you had to compromise or let go
followUps: Looking back, is there anything you'd have dropped sooner?

question: Describe a significant setback you experienced outside of school. What did it teach you?
lookingFor: Resilience drawn from real life — the ability to process difficulty and extract something meaningful from it.

bullets: Choose something you can speak about with honesty, without oversharing
Show how you processed it rather than just that you got through it
Name a belief or habit that came out of the experience
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My family moved countries when I was sixteen, and I had to rebuild almost everything socially in a new language. There were months where I felt invisible because I couldn't express myself with any nuance. What I did was join a debate club specifically because it forced me to speak out loud every week, even badly. Over time that discomfort became something I actively looked for rather than avoided. I now genuinely believe that feeling out of your depth is usually a sign you're in the right room.

commonMistakes: Describing hardship as purely negative without showing any growth or agency
Choosing something so personal it becomes hard to keep professional
Presenting the lesson as trivially obvious rather than hard-won
followUps: How does that experience shape how you approach new and unfamiliar situations now?

subtopic: A goal you did not reach
question: Tell us about a goal you set and did not achieve. What happened?
lookingFor: Honest reflection on unmet goals — insight into how the applicant sets, pursues, and learns from ambitious targets.

bullets: Name the goal clearly and explain why it mattered
Be specific about why you fell short — not vague
Show what you adjusted as a result
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I wanted to read fifty books in a year. I got to thirty-one and stopped because I'd started choosing short, easy books just to keep my count up, and it stopped being meaningful. Looking back, the target was the wrong shape — I'd optimised for quantity and quietly abandoned the point. The next year I picked twelve books I'd been putting off because they looked hard, and I actually finished all of them. I learned that goals need a why attached, not just a number.

commonMistakes: Picking a goal so trivial the panel doesn't care whether you hit it
Blaming external reasons while ignoring your own choices
Not connecting the failure to any change in how you set goals now
followUps: Do you think the original goal was realistic, or was there a problem with the target itself?

question: How do you perform under pressure? Can you give us an example?
lookingFor: A realistic, self-aware account of how the applicant actually functions under pressure — not a claim to thrive effortlessly.

bullets: Acknowledge pressure is real — don't claim immunity to it
Give a specific example with a concrete outcome
Name the habit or technique that helps you function
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I don't love pressure, but I've learned to work with it. During a mock exam season where three papers fell on consecutive days, I realised that trying to revise right up until each exam made me worse, not better. I started giving myself an hour of nothing before each paper — a walk, or just sitting somewhere quiet. My scores were more consistent that week than at any point that year. Pressure still affects me, but I know now that my job is to protect my thinking, not to perform heroics at the last minute.

commonMistakes: Claiming pressure has no effect on you, which sounds implausible
Describing pure panic with no coping mechanism
Giving an example with no clear outcome
followUps: Is there a type of pressure you still find particularly difficult?

subtopic: Persisting when you wanted to quit
question: Tell us about a time you seriously considered giving something up. What made you keep going?
lookingFor: Authentic grit — not a claim to never doubt, but evidence of a reasoned choice to persist and what it produced.

bullets: Make the temptation to quit feel real, not theatrical
Show that the decision to continue was a genuine choice, not just stubbornness
End with what the persistence produced — concrete, not vague
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I nearly quit my extended project halfway through. I'd picked a question that turned out to be much harder to research than I'd expected, and by month four I had a folder of conflicting sources and no argument. I drafted an email to my supervisor saying I wanted to change topics. I didn't send it. Instead I gave myself one more week to find a single clear claim I could actually defend. I found it, rebuilt from there, and the project ended up being the piece of work I'm proudest of. Quitting at the right time is sometimes wise, but I'm glad I gave it one more week first.

commonMistakes: Making it sound like there was never any real doubt
Framing it as pure willpower with nothing that actually helped
Describing a situation where quitting would clearly have been the right call
followUps: How do you decide when persistence is right and when cutting your losses is the wiser move?

question: Describe a time you were really disappointed by an outcome. How did you deal with it?
lookingFor: Emotional honesty combined with a healthy recovery process — neither denial nor prolonged dwelling.

bullets: Let the disappointment be real and specific
Show a recovery process, not an instant bounce-back
Describe what you did rather than how you felt
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I auditioned for the lead role in our school play and was given a minor supporting part. I was honestly upset for a few days, and I let myself be upset rather than pretending I was fine. Then I made a decision: I'd treat the smaller role as a technical exercise and do it as precisely as I could. I ended up getting some of the best feedback I'd ever received, and the director told me later it was that performance that made her cast me in a larger role the following year. The disappointment led somewhere, but only because I processed it rather than buried it.

commonMistakes: Pretending the disappointment barely registered
Dwelling on the unfairness of the outcome rather than the response
Not showing a clear next step after the initial reaction
followUps: Do you think disappointment is something that gets easier to handle, or is it always the same?

question: Tell us about a time a team project went wrong. What did you do, and what did you learn?
lookingFor: The ability to take shared ownership of group failure — and to contribute to a fix rather than assign blame.

bullets: Show ownership of your own role in what went wrong, not just the team's
Describe the specific action you took to help recover the situation
Extract a lesson that's about process, not just 'choose better teammates'
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A group I was in for a history coursework piece divided up the research too cleanly — we each took a section and almost never talked until the week before the deadline. By then we had four good individual pieces that didn't fit together at all. I suggested an emergency session where we read each other's work aloud and mapped the overlaps. It wasn't pretty, but we produced a coherent submission. My lesson was that I'd assumed coordination would happen naturally, and from then on I've treated early check-ins as a non-negotiable part of any group work.

commonMistakes: Framing the problem as entirely other people's fault
Describing what went wrong at length without showing what you personally did
A lesson that's too generic to be useful
followUps: What would you put in place at the start of a group project to prevent that from happening?

question: Tell us about a time plans changed at the last minute and you had to adapt. How did you handle it?
lookingFor: Practical flexibility and composure — evidence the applicant can problem-solve under conditions they didn't choose.

bullets: Set up the unexpected change quickly and specifically
Show the decision-making in the moment, not just the outcome
Reflect briefly on what the experience revealed about how you work
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Our speaker for an event I was organising pulled out the morning of the event — about four hours before it was due to start. Rather than panic, I spent thirty minutes listing every option I had and picked the one that was most achievable: asking a teacher who'd done related work to run a Q&A instead. The session was unscripted and turned out to be the best-reviewed part of the day. I learned I have a reasonably steady head in a crisis, and that sometimes a plan B works better than plan A.

commonMistakes: Describing the chaos but not the decision-making process
Making the adaptation sound effortless when it clearly wasn't
Missing the reflective beat at the end
followUps: How do you tend to feel just after an unexpected disruption — what's your first instinct?

question: Describe a time you had to ask for help. What made it difficult, and what happened?
lookingFor: Intellectual humility and the maturity to recognise the limits of self-reliance — both important in a demanding course.

bullets: Acknowledge why asking for help felt hard — don't pretend it was simple
Show specifically what you asked for and from whom
Reflect on what shifted in how you now approach needing help
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I struggled silently with a physics unit for about three weeks before I finally went to my teacher. I'd convinced myself that asking meant I wasn't clever enough to be doing A-level Physics. When I finally went, she explained the concept in about ten minutes and told me several other students had the same gap. I'd wasted three weeks protecting my ego. Since then I've set myself a rule: if I'm stuck on something for more than two days, I go and ask. The course I'm applying for is demanding and I know asking early is the strategy that works.

commonMistakes: Making asking for help sound easy when it was clearly uncomfortable
Choosing a trivial example where the stakes were low
Not showing how your approach to asking for help changed
followUps: Is there still a situation where you'd find it hard to ask for help?

question: Tell us about a mistake you made that had real consequences. How did you handle it?
lookingFor: Clear accountability — honest about what went wrong, direct about consequences, focused on repair rather than damage control.

bullets: Own the mistake cleanly, without softening it into a non-mistake
Describe the real consequences without minimising them
Show you took action to repair what you could, not just apologise
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was responsible for submitting a group's coursework draft to our teacher for feedback, and I attached the wrong version — an earlier draft that hadn't incorporated everyone's edits. We got feedback on work that wasn't our best, and two of my group members were understandably frustrated. I didn't try to explain it away. I told our teacher what had happened, asked if she'd be willing to look at the correct version briefly, and spent the next two days helping the group recover the ground. She wasn't able to re-mark it, but we used her comments on the right version for the final submission and it turned out well. I learned to double-check before I send anything on behalf of others.

commonMistakes: Softening the mistake so much it no longer sounds like one
Over-explaining circumstances as a way of distributing blame
Stopping at the apology without describing action taken
followUps: What did the people affected think of how you handled it?

question: Have you ever been in a situation where you felt you were being treated unfairly? How did you respond?
lookingFor: Emotional regulation and constructive action — the ability to advocate without becoming reactive or self-pitying.

bullets: Be specific about what felt unfair without dwelling on grievance
Show what constructive action you took
Acknowledge what you can and cannot control in such situations
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A piece of work I submitted was marked significantly lower than similar work by a classmate, and when I compared them I thought the difference was hard to justify. I didn't complain informally to anyone — I booked time with the teacher and asked her to walk me through the marking criteria and where specifically my piece had fallen short. She explained a structural point I'd genuinely missed, and while I still thought the gap was larger than warranted, I could see there was a real argument. I didn't get a remark, but I understood the feedback and used it. I'd rather understand than be right.

commonMistakes: Dwelling on the injustice and losing sight of the constructive response
Claiming you were completely in the right and the other party was wrong
Describing an inappropriate escalation without reflection
followUps: What would you do if the same thing happened and the teacher's explanation still didn't satisfy you?

question: Have you ever been criticised or corrected in front of others? How did you handle it in the moment?
lookingFor: Grace under public correction — the ability to receive criticism without shutting down or reacting defensively.

bullets: Admit the initial emotional impact without dwelling on it
Show you stayed present and engaged rather than withdrawing
Reflect on what you did with the criticism afterwards
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: During a rehearsal for a public speech competition, the coach stopped me mid-sentence and said my pacing was making it hard to follow my argument. About fifteen people were watching. My face went red, which I couldn't control, but I took a breath and said 'okay — where should I slow down?' She pointed to three places, I tried again, and she said it was better. Afterwards I was glad she did it publicly because I had to keep going rather than retreat. I used the same correction the next day in the actual competition and it helped.

commonMistakes: Pretending public criticism didn't sting when it clearly would have
Describing a defensive reaction without acknowledging it as one
Focusing only on the embarrassment rather than the action taken
followUps: Do you think there's a difference between how you receive private criticism and public correction?

question: Tell us about a time you prepared thoroughly for something but it still didn't go the way you'd planned.
lookingFor: Honesty that preparation has limits, and the maturity to adapt in the moment rather than blame the outcome.

bullets: Show that the preparation was genuine, not an excuse for the outcome
Describe what was outside your preparation and how you responded in the moment
Extract a nuanced lesson — not just 'prepare more'
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I prepared for a Model UN conference more thoroughly than anything I'd done before — I knew the position paper cold, had counter-arguments ready, and had practiced speaking. On the day, the bloc dynamics shifted completely in the first hour: two delegations I'd planned to work with aligned against us. I had to improvise a new strategy mid-session and ended up forming an alliance I hadn't anticipated. We got a resolution through. The lesson wasn't that preparation is overrated — it's that preparation gives you a platform, not a guarantee. The real skill is what you do when the platform shifts.

commonMistakes: Using the story as evidence that preparation isn't worth it
Blaming external factors for the deviation from the plan
Not showing any adaptive thinking in the moment
followUps: Was there anything you could have prepared for that you'd missed, or was the disruption genuinely unforeseeable?

question: Have you ever been rejected from something — a team, a role, a competition? How did you respond?
lookingFor: A healthy response to rejection — processing it, seeking feedback, and continuing without becoming bitter or passive.

bullets: Name the rejection clearly — be specific, not vague
Show that you sought to understand why, not just to feel better
Describe what you did next that was driven by the rejection
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I applied to write for our school's online magazine and was turned down. My first reaction was to close the email and not think about it. A day later I emailed the editor and asked what hadn't worked in my submission. She gave me specific feedback on structure and said my voice was strong but I'd buried my argument. I rewrote the piece with that in mind, submitted it again three months later when they opened submissions, and it was published. The rejection only became useful once I decided to treat it as information.

commonMistakes: Describing a rejection without any active response — just moving on
Focusing on the injustice of being rejected rather than what you did with it
Not being specific about the rejection or the steps taken
followUps: Do you think rejection gets easier over time, or does it always sting as much?

subtopic: Changing course after a wrong decision
question: Tell us about a time you realised you'd made the wrong decision and had to change course. What did you do?
lookingFor: The intellectual honesty to recognise a wrong turn and the decisiveness to correct it — without excessive self-flagellation.

bullets: Be honest that you initially resisted seeing it as a wrong decision
Show the specific moment you recognised it needed to change
Describe the correction and what it cost or took
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I chose my dissertation topic for an extended project based on what sounded impressive rather than what I was genuinely curious about. Two months in I had a folder of research I didn't care about and writing that felt like teeth-pulling. I had a blunt conversation with myself and switched topics — which meant starting again, losing six weeks of work. The second topic was something I'd actually been thinking about for years: how music notation shapes how composers think. The work came easily and I finished early. The lesson is to notice the early signs of misalignment rather than wait until you're in too deep.

commonMistakes: Framing the original decision as obviously wrong from the start
Minimising what the course correction cost or required
Not showing what told you that the decision needed to change
followUps: How do you now distinguish early signs of a wrong decision from normal early-stage difficulty?

question: Tell us about a challenge you had to face largely on your own, without much support around you.
lookingFor: Self-reliance and the ability to resource yourself when conventional support isn't available.

bullets: Make clear why the support was limited — not as an excuse but as context
Show what you did to sustain yourself through it
Connect it to something you can now do independently that you couldn't before
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I taught myself the basics of video editing for a documentary project at school when my teacher had limited expertise in it and there was no one to troubleshoot with. I spent most of two weekends on tutorial forums, working through error messages one by one. There were moments of pure frustration, but there was also something satisfying about solving each problem with no one to hand the answer to me. By the end I had a skill I hadn't had before, and more importantly I now trust that I can learn technical tools when I need to, even when there's no one to guide me.

commonMistakes: Framing isolation as purely a hardship with no upside
Not specifying what you actually did to work through it
Choosing an example so minor that facing it alone is not impressive
followUps: Was there a point where you considered seeking outside help? What stopped you?

subtopic: Maintaining focus during a difficult period
question: Has there been a period in your life when it was genuinely difficult to focus on your studies? How did you manage?
lookingFor: Candour about life's interference with study, paired with practical strategies and self-awareness — not a claim to perfect focus.

bullets: Be honest about the difficulty without over-disclosing private details
Show a real strategy you used, not just willpower
Acknowledge the limits of what you managed and what you learned from that
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: During a period when a family member was seriously unwell, concentrating on work was genuinely hard. I didn't pretend otherwise to my teachers, which felt uncomfortable but was the right call — it meant they were patient when submissions were late. What helped me most was working in short, defined blocks of thirty minutes rather than trying to sit for two hours and achieving nothing. I also accepted that some weeks the studying would just be maintenance, not progress, and that was enough. My grades dipped slightly but recovered. The main thing I learned is that transparency with people who can actually help — teachers, not everyone — matters more than keeping up appearances.

commonMistakes: Claiming to have maintained perfect focus despite serious difficulty
Over-disclosing personal details about others
Framing the period as entirely outside their control with nothing they could do
followUps: Looking back, is there anything you wish you'd done differently during that time?

subtopic: Taking on something beyond your skill level
question: Tell us about a time you took on something you were not sure you could do. What happened?
lookingFor: Intellectual courage and a growth mindset — the willingness to stretch and to be a beginner in front of others.

bullets: Show that the stretch was genuine, not performative
Describe the specific difficulty of being out of your depth
End with what you can now do that you couldn't before
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I volunteered to run the programming track at our school's tech fair despite having taught myself only the basics of Python. I was genuinely unsure if I could hold the room for two hours. I over-prepared the fundamentals and was honest with the students on the day that some questions might take me a moment to think through. A few did. But being honest about that seemed to make the session more relaxed rather than less, and several students said it was the most interactive part of the day. I learned more preparing to teach it than I had learning it for myself.

commonMistakes: Choosing an example where you were actually quite confident from the start
Not describing the discomfort of not knowing enough
A vague outcome — it 'went well' without specifics
followUps: What made you volunteer rather than wait until you felt more ready?

subtopic: Failure to meet your own standards
question: Describe a time when you produced work you were genuinely unhappy with. What did you do about it?
lookingFor: High personal standards combined with the pragmatism to learn from disappointing output rather than be paralysed by it.

bullets: Be specific about why the work fell short of your own standard
Show what action you took — not just how you felt about it
Reflect on what the experience changed about how you work
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I submitted a philosophy essay I knew wasn't my best thinking because I ran out of time to revise it properly. It came back with a decent mark but comments that I'd been superficial in places — which was exactly what I'd worried about when I submitted it. I read the marker's notes carefully and wrote a revised version, not for a mark but to work through the argument properly. That sounds odd, but it helped: I understood the question better by the end than I had at submission. I now build in a revision day before any deadline rather than treating it as optional.

commonMistakes: Describing disappointment with a grade rather than with your own work
Showing only the emotional response with no action
Framing a high mark as adequate — the panel wants to see your standards, not the teacher's
followUps: How do you judge when a piece of work is genuinely done versus when you're just being hard on yourself?

subtopic: When you caused a problem for others
question: Describe a time your actions — even unintentionally — caused problems for someone else. What did you do?
lookingFor: Accountability without defensiveness — and the willingness to prioritise repair over self-justification.

bullets: Acknowledge your role clearly, even if it was unintentional
Show that you prioritised the other person's situation, not your own discomfort
Describe what you did to repair it and what it cost you
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I shared some information in a group chat that I thought was common knowledge, and it turned out it wasn't — it was something a friend had told me privately. She found out and was upset. I didn't try to explain that I hadn't meant to do it, because that felt beside the point. I apologised directly, acknowledged I should have checked before sharing, and gave her space to be annoyed. It took a few weeks to repair, and I didn't rush it. I now treat anything told to me in a one-to-one conversation as private by default unless I've been told otherwise.

commonMistakes: Centring the explanation of your intentions rather than the impact
A quick apology that skips over the actual repair
Suggesting the other person overreacted
followUps: Was there a moment where you were tempted to justify yourself rather than just apologise?

subtopic: How you handle not knowing the answer
question: How do you handle being in a situation — in class, in a discussion, anywhere — where you simply don't know the answer?
lookingFor: Intellectual honesty and curiosity over performance — the ability to sit with not knowing and use it productively.

bullets: Show that not knowing doesn't destabilise you
Describe a habit or process you use when you're stuck
Demonstrate genuine curiosity — not knowing should feel like a pull, not a failure
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My honest reaction used to be to go quiet and hope someone else answered. What shifted it was a teacher who said 'I don't know' in class and then worked through where she'd find out — and it was the most interesting part of the lesson. Now when I don't know something I try to say so and think aloud about where to look: is it a fact I can check, a concept I can trace back, or a genuine open question? I actually find genuinely open questions more interesting than ones with known answers. University will have a lot more of those, and that's partly why I want to go.

commonMistakes: Claiming to always know enough or to look it up immediately without saying more
Describing paralysis or embarrassment with no recovery
Not connecting the answer to intellectual curiosity — it should sound like you find this energising
followUps: Can you think of a question in your subject that you genuinely don't know the answer to — one that interests you?
